18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]

117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.

121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]

131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE (iter-007): blocked on absent tensor/monoidal structure for SheafOfModules.
160	  % The degree-m component is Gamma(X_s, L_s^{otimes m}); Mathlib at the pinned commit has no
161	  % MonoidalCategoryStruct on X.Modules (synthesis fails) and no SheafOfModules/PresheafOfModules
162	  % tensor lemmas, so L^{otimes m} cannot even be named. This is a deep infrastructure prerequisite
163	  % (tensor product of sheaves of modules + lax-monoidal global sections), to be blueprinted as a
164	  % separate multi-lemma sub-build. See task_results .../QuotScheme.md.
165	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
166	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
167	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
168	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
169	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
170	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
171	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
172	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
173	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
174	  %  the function
175	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
176	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
177	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
178	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
179	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
180	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
181	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
182	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
183	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
184	  \[
185	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
186	  \]
187	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
188	  the tensor product of sections
189	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
190	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
191	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
192	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
193	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
194	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
195	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
196	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
197	  polynomial ring).
198	\end{definition}

200	\begin{definition}
201	[Section graded module]
202	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
203	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
204	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
205	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
206	  % Polynomials" (read from
207	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
208	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
209	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
210	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
211	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
212	  %  the function
213	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
214	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
215	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
216	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
217	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
218	  I.7.}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
220	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
221	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
222	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
223	  \[
224	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
225	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
226	  \]
227	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
228	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
229	  by the section tensor product
230	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
231	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
232	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
233	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
234	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
235	\end{definition}

237	\begin{lemma}
238	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
239	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
241	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
242	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
243	  % Polynomials" (read from
244	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
245	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
246	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
247	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
248	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
249	  %  the function
250	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
251	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
252	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
253	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
254	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
255	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
256	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
257	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
258	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
259	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
260	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
261	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
262	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
263	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
264	\end{lemma}
265	
266	\begin{proof}
267	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
268	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
269	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
270	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
271	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
272	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
273	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
274	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
275	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
276	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
277	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
278	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
279	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
280	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
281	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
282	\end{proof}

284	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
285	  graded series]
286	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
287	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
288	  \mathlibok
289	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
290	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
291	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
292	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
293	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
294	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
295	  equals \(h(n)\):
296	  \[
297	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
298	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
299	  \]
300	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
301	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
302	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
303	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
304	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
305	\end{lemma}

307	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
308	  (rank--nullity)]
309	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
310	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
311	  \mathlibok
312	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
313	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
314	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
315	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
316	  \[
317	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
318	  \]
319	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
320	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
321	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
322	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
323	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
324	\end{lemma}

326	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
327	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
328	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
329	  \mathlibok
330	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
331	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
332	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a

336	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}

341	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert

343	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by

434	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
435	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
436	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
437	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
438	%  following short exact sequence
439	%  $$
440	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
441	%  $$
442	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
443	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
444	\begin{proof}
445	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
446	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
447	    lem:ratHilb_bump}
448	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
449	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
450	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
451	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
452	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
453	
454	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
455	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
456	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
457	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
458	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
459	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). Then \(R\) is a graded
460	  quotient of a polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) with the \(x_i\) of
461	  degree \(1\); we induct on the minimal number \(r\) of degree-one generators.
462	
463	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} Here \(R = \kappa\) and \(M\) is a finitely
464	  generated \(\kappa\)-module, hence finite-dimensional, so \(M_n = 0\) and
465	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for all \(n \gg 0\). The Hilbert series
466	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is therefore a polynomial; taking \(d = 0\)
467	  and \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) the polynomial with these coefficients gives
468	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = [X^n]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{0}\bigr)\) for all \(n\).
469	
470	  \emph{Inductive step.} Let \(x = x_r \in R_1\) be one of a minimal set of
471	  degree-one generators, so that the induction hypothesis applies to finitely
472	  generated graded modules over \(R/(x)\), itself a graded quotient of
473	  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_{r-1}]\). Multiplication by \(x\) raises degree by one
474	  and assembles, for every \(n\), into a short exact sequence of
475	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector spaces
476	  \[
477	    0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{\;x\;} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0,
478	  \]
479	  where \(K = \ker\bigl(x : M \to M(1)\bigr)\) and \(C = M / xM\) are finitely
480	  generated graded \(R\)-modules annihilated by \(x\), hence finitely generated
481	  graded modules over \(R/(x)\). By additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact
482	  sequence (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}, applied to the kernel and
483	  cokernel of the map \(x\)),
484	  \[
485	    \dim_\kappa M_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa M_n
486	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n
487	  \]
488	  for every \(n\). Multiplying the Hilbert series of \(M\) by \((1 - X)\) and
489	  summing this identity against \(X^{n}\) shows that
490	  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) equals the Hilbert series of
491	  \(C\) minus that of \(K\), up to a polynomial in low degrees. By the induction
492	  hypothesis applied to the \(R/(x)\)-modules \(K\) and \(C\) (which live over
493	  \(r - 1\) variables), each of those Hilbert series is, up to a polynomial, of
494	  the form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\) with \(d - 1 \le r - 1\). Hence
495	  \[
496	    (1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n
497	      \;=\; q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)} \qquad \text{up to a polynomial,}
498	  \]
499	  and multiplying by the inverse power series
500	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1) = (1 - X)^{-1}\)
501	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) gives
502	  \[
503	    \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n
504	      \;=\; p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \qquad \text{up to a polynomial,}
505	  \]
506	  with \(d \le r\). Reading off the coefficient of \(X^n\) for \(n \gg 0\) yields
507	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) with
508	  \(\dim_\kappa M_n = [X^n]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\),
509	  which is the claim.
510	\end{proof}

512	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
513	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
514	
515	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
516	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
517	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
518	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
519	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
520	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
521	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
522	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
523	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
524	
525	\begin{lemma}[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
526	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
527	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
528	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
529	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
530	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
531	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
532	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
533	  \(F\):
534	  \[
535	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
536	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
537	  \]
538	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
539	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
540	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
541	  the Cauchy product.
542	\end{lemma}
543	
544	\begin{lemma}[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
545	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
546	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
547	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
548	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
549	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
550	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
551	  \[
552	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
553	  \]
554	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
555	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
556	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
557	  \[
558	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
559	  \]
560	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
561	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
562	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
563	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
564	\end{lemma}
565	
566	\begin{proof}
567	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
568	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
569	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
570	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
571	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
572	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
573	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
574	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
575	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
576	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
577	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
578	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
579	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
580	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
581	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
582	\end{proof}
583	
584	\begin{definition}[Rational Hilbert function]
585	  \label{def:ratHilb}
586	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
587	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
588	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
589	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
590	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
591	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
592	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
593	  \[
594	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
595	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
596	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
597	  \]
598	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
599	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
600	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
601	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
602	\end{definition}
603	
604	\begin{lemma}[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
605	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
606	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
607	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
608	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
609	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
610	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
611	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
612	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
613	\end{lemma}
614	
615	\begin{lemma}[Raising the pole order]
616	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
617	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
618	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
619	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
620	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
621	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
622	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
623	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
624	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
625	\end{lemma}
626	
627	\begin{lemma}[Closure under difference]
628	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
629	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
630	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
631	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
632	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
633	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
634	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
635	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
636	\end{lemma}
637	
638	\begin{lemma}[Closure under right shift]
639	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
640	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
641	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
642	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
643	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
644	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
645	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
646	  Lean.
647	\end{lemma}
648	
649	\begin{lemma}[Antidifference step for the predicate]
650	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
651	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
652	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
653	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
654	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
655	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
656	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
657	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
658	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
659	\end{lemma}
660	
661	\begin{proof}
662	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
663	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
664	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
665	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
666	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
667	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
668	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
669	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
670	\end{proof}
671	
672	\begin{lemma}[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
673	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
674	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
675	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
676	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
677	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
678	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
679	  \[
680	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
681	  \]
682	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
683	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
684	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
685	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
686	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
687	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
688	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
689	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
690	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
691	\end{lemma}
692	
693	\begin{proof}
694	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
695	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
696	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
697	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
698	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
699	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
700	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
701	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
702	\end{proof}

704	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
705	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
706	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
707	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
708	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
709	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
710	  % Polynomials" (read from
711	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
712	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
713	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
714	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
715	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
716	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
717	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
718	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
719	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
720	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
721	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
722	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
723	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
724	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
725	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
726	  \[
727	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
728	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
729	  \]
730	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
731	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
732	\end{theorem}
733	
734	\begin{proof}
735	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
736	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
737	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
738	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
739	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
740	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
741	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
742	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
743	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
744	  function
745	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
746	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
747	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
748	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
749	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
750	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
751	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
752	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
753	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
754	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
755	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
756	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
757	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
758	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
759	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
760	\end{proof}

762	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
763	\label{sec:quot_predicates}

765	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
766	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
767	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
768	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
769	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.

773	\leanok

794	  project-built primitive.}
795	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
796	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
797	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
798	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
799	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
800	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
801	  \(U \subseteq X\),
802	  \[
803	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
804	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
805	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
806	  \]
807	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
808	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
809	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
810	
811	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
812	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
813	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
814	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
815	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
816	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
817	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
818	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
819	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
820	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
821	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
822	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
823	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
824	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
825	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
826	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
827	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
828	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
829	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
830	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
831	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
832	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.

859	\begin{lemma}\leanok
860	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
861	  localization]
862	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
863	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
864	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
865	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
866	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
867	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
868	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
869	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
870	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
871	  \[
872	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
873	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
874	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
875	  \]
876	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
877	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
878	
879	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
880	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
881	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
882	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
883	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
884	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
885	\end{lemma}
886	
887	\begin{proof}
888	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
889	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
890	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
891	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
892	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
893	
894	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
895	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
896	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
897	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
898	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
899	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
900	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
901	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
902	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
903	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
904	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
905	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
906	\end{proof}

1359	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
1360	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
1361	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
1362	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
1363	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
1364	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
1365	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
1366	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
1367	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
1368	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
1369	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
1370	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
1371	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
1372	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
1373	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
1374	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
1375	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
1376	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
1377	
1378	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
1379	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
1380	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
1381	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
1382	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
1383	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
1384	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
